coredata.dc:title: Polymerized carbon dots with high electrochemiluminescence efficiency and long wavelength ECL emission for ultrasensitive detection of MicroRNA-222
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Shuang
Yang, Yu-Ting
Chen, Yi-Fei
Liu, Jia-Li
Chai, Ya-Qin
Yuan, Ruo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a new electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor was constructed with highly efficient polymerized carbon dots (PCDs) as ECL emitter and the improved localized catalytic hairpin assembly (L-CHA) as signal amplifier for ultrasensitive detection of microRNA-222 (miRNA-222). Impressively, compared to the traditional carbon dots with inefficient blue region ECL emission, PCDs with N, O co-dope and large conjugated π-system showed high electrical conductivity, narrow band gap and strong radiative transition, which could exhibit high ECL efficiency to improve the sensitivity of detection and long wavelength ECL emission to achieve deep tissue penetration for reducing biological damage. Furthermore, the trace target miRNA-222 could be efficiently converted into large amounts of output DNA labelled with the quencher dopamine (S-DA) through the L-CHA reaction to significantly enhance the target amplification efficiency for further improving the sensitivity of detection. Thus, the ECL biosensor could achieve the ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-222 from 100 aM to 100 pM with the detection limit of 76 aM. Therefore, this work proposed a novel CDs with high ECL efficiency and long wavelength ECL emission, which not only was used to build an ultrasensitive biosensor for biomolecules detection in clinical diagnosis, but also served as a potential emitter for ECL bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymerized carbon dots
Long wavelength ECL emission
Localized catalytic hairpin assembly
microRNA-222

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Signal amplification of the localized catalytic hairpin assembly (L-CHA)
Morphology characterization of the prepared PCDs
Enhancement mechanism of ECL binary (PCDs/S2O8 2−) system
The feasibility analysis of L-CHA strategy
Performance of the biosensor to detect miRNA-222
Application of designed biosensors in practical samples

Chongqing Yousheng technology development Co. LTD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image the scale of (A) 20 nm (inset: distribution image of particle size), (B) 10 nm (inset: HR-TEM), and (C) AFM image of PCDs. (D) The height profile (AFM) of PCDs.
(A) XPS survey of PCDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, and (D) O 1s.

(A) FT-IR spectra of a) CDs and b) PCDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of a) OP -CDs, b) DA -CDs and c) PCDs. (the inset photograph of PCDs is under ultraviolet lamp). (C) FL and ECL spectra of PCDs. (D) ECL 3D color map of PCDs in 0.1 M PBS with 50 mM S2O8 2−. (E) ECL responses and (F) CV curves of GCE in PBS (curve a) and in 10 mM S2O8 2− (curve b), PCDs/GCE in PBS (curve c) and in10 mM S2O8 2− (curve d).
PAGE analysis of different samples. The concentration of every sample is 1 μM.

(A) ECL biosensors response of various miRNA-222 concentrations from 100 pM to 100 aM. (n = 3). (B) Calibration curve of miRNA-222 detection. (C) The selectivity study of the biosensor with interferents (a) blank, (b) miRNA-21, (c) miRNA-141, (d) miRNA-221, (e) mixed sample, (f) miRNA-222. Error bars, SD, n = 3. (D) The stability investigation of the biosensor. (E) Corresponding ECL signal of the biosensor in cells lysates (HeLa and MHCC-97L) at cells concentrations from 1 × 101 (a) to 1 × 104 (e). (F). qRT-PCR detecting miRNA-222 in different cell lysates (MHCC-97L, HeLa).

(A) The preparation of PCDs with long wavelength ECL emission (B) The meliorative localized catalytic hairpin amplification strategy (L-CHA) for the conversion efficiency of the target miRNA-222, and (C) ECL biosensor construction process and ECL signal response.

head.ce:title: Polymerized carbon dots with high electrochemiluminescence efficiency and long wavelength ECL emission for ultrasensitive detection of MicroRNA-222

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical EngQneering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical EngQneering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical EngQneering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a new electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor was constructed with highly efficient polymerized carbon dots (PCDs) as ECL emitter and the improved localized catalytic hairpin assembly (L-CHA) as signal amplifier for ultrasensitive detection of microRNA-222 (miRNA-222). Impressively, compared to the traditional carbon dots with inefficient blue region ECL emission, PCDs with N, O co-dope and large conjugated π-system showed high electrical conductivity, narrow band gap and strong radiative transition, which could exhibit high ECL efficiency to improve the sensitivity of detection and long wavelength ECL emission to achieve deep tissue penetration for reducing biological damage. Furthermore, the trace target miRNA-222 could be efficiently converted into large amounts of output DNA labelled with the quencher dopamine (S-DA) through the L-CHA reaction to significantly enhance the target amplification efficiency for further improving the sensitivity of detection. Thus, the ECL biosensor could achieve the ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-222 from 100 aM to 100 pM with the detection limit of 76 aM. Therefore, this work proposed a novel CDs with high ECL efficiency and long wavelength ECL emission, which not only was used to build an ultrasensitive biosensor for biomolecules detection in clinical diagnosis, but also served as a potential emitter for ECL bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) combining the controllability of electrochemistry and the sensitivity of chemiluminescence has obvious merits of low background signal, high sensitivity and short detection time (Wang et al., 2023a; Ru et al., 2023). Currently, the nanomaterials (organic nanocrystals, metal nanoclusters and quantum dot etc.) (Sun et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023a; Xia et al., 2022) with large specific surface area, outstanding optical properties and excellent catalytic properties have been explored as ECL emitters to improve the practical application in ECL fields. Interestingly, carbon dots (CDs) as zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials have the advantages of small particle size, easy functionalization, optical adjustability, excellent biocompatibility and good stability, which is widely used as luminophore to build a biosensor for detecting biomolecules (Liu et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2023). However, currently most CDs synthesized from small molecules and bioresources caused unstable ECL signal and short ECL wavelength, resulting in the poor repeatability, bad controllability and severe biological damage of the designed biosensor (Guo et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2022; Qin et al., 2023). On account of the above situation, we put forward a high-efficient polymerized CDs (PCDs) synthesized from aromatic compound by bottom-up method, which is made up of a carbon core and the connected polymer chains linked many functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups). Thus, PCDs with high ECL efficiency and long wavelength emission ascribed to the introduction of additional surface states by N, O co-doping and the formation of large conjugated π-system by polymerization reaction, which increased the electron transition, reduced the band gap and increased the radiation transition (X.W. Gao et al., 2022; Panigrahi et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023a).

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are a class of non-coding RNA regulating gene expression (Peng et al., 2023), among which the aberrant expression of miRNA-222 is closely associated with the new onset diabetes (Kim et al., 2021), liver cancer (Yang et al., 2023) and papillary thyroid cancer (Viviana et al., 2023), etc. Therefore, it is notably important to quantitatively detect the abundance of miRNA-222 for early diagnosis of the disease. In current clinical diagnosis, the expression level of miRNA can be detected by northern blotting, real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) and DNA microarrays (Martins et al., 2022; Nam et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023b). However, the above detection methods still suffer from certain limitations of high equipment cost, time consuming and high background signal. Hence, it's very urgent to propose a low-background and ultrasensitive method to detect miRNA-222. Nevertheless, the low abundance of miRNA in the early judgment of disease makes it difficult to achieve the early diagnosis of the disease. Therefore, nucleic acid amplification strategies (hybridization chain reaction (HCR), catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA), rolling circle amplification (RCA) (Wang et al., 2023b; Zhou et al., 2023), etc.) have been proposed to effectively improve the target conversion efficiency for further improving the sensitivity of detection. Compared with enzyme amplification strategies, the enzyme-free amplification strategies overcome the defects of high spending, environmental sensitivity, complex operation, etc (Li et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2023). Thus, CHA with simple operation and high target utilization rate as the enzyme-free amplification strategy has attracted much attention from researchers. In this work, the meliorative localized catalytic hairpin assembly (L-CHA) was proposed to improve the reaction rate by increasing the local concentration of hairpin and limiting the reaction space for the high target amplification efficiency.

Herein, a "signal-on-off" mode ECL biosensor was constructed by using PCDs as ECL luminophore and improved L-CHA as signal amplifier to detect low abundance of miRNA-222 from the lysates of early tumor cells. First, as shown in Scheme 1 A, PCDs with a strong and stable long wavelength ECL emission was synthesized with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and dopamine hydrochloride (DA) through one-step hydrothermal method. L-CHA reaction was triggered by the target miRNA-222 (T) to produce a large amount of output DNA (S-DA) for developing the biosensor (Scheme 1B). Specifically, three single stranded DNA (H1), DNA (L) assembled on MBs and DNA (S) labelled with the quencher dopamine (S-DA) were annealed together to form a composite structure (MBs-L-H1-S-DA). Then, the T as the initiator was introduced to open the hairpin H1 for the exposure of the toehold of H1. Furthermore, with the participation of hairpin H2, the sticky ends of H1 reacted with H2 to catalyze the hairpin self-assembly process for simultaneously releasing the T and S-DA. Interestingly, T can react directly with adjacent H1 on L to execute L-CHA process for producing a large amount of S-DA. Ultimately, a large amount of S-DA was produced through the L-CHA reaction. Finally, as seen in Scheme 1C, PCDs provided a strong ECL response ("signal on" state), then the capture DNA (cp DNA) incubated on the electrode hybridized with S-DA generated from the above L-CHA reaction to weaken the ECL response because of the quenching effect of DA (“signal-off” state). Therefore, the designed biosensor revealed excellent detection performance for miRNA-222 with detection limits as low as 76 aM, which was further used in the lysate of cancer cells (MHCC-97L) to detect miRNA-222. It is worth noting that the proposed biosensor based on the PCDs with long wavelength ECL emission and localized CHA is expected to be more widely applied in the detection of biomolecules, early diagnosis of diseases and other fields in the future.

Polymerized carbon dots (PCDs) were synthesized according to a previous study with some modifications (Lu et al., 2017). First, 306 mg of dopamine hydrochloride (DAH) and 180 mg of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were dissolved in 16.67 mL deionized water, and 500 μL of hydrochloric acid (HCl, pH optimization was described in the supporting information Fig. S1) was dropped into the above solution. After agitation, the solution was added to 50 mL of autoclave and heated in a 200 °C oven for 8 h. Then, the reactor was cooled to about 25 °C to remove the obtained ink black liquid. Subsequently, the reactor was soaked with 15 mL of ethanol for 12 h. Next, the ethanol solution was purified by a dialysis bag (cutoff molecular weight: 3500 Da) with deionized water in refrigerator at 4 °C for two days and the deionized water was displaced every 4 h. Finally, the PCDs was collected and stored away from light.

In order to prepare pinwheel-shaped DNA structure, the following experiments were carried out in this work. First, the single stranded DNA (L) was annealed at 95 °C for 5 min and then quickly cooled to 25 °C to form a linear chain. Subsequently, 10 μL of COOH-modified magnetic beads (MBs) was blended into 40 μL of N-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 0.1M) and shaken for 15 min to activate carboxyl. Next, 10 μL of N-hydroxy succinimide solution (NHS, 0.1M) and10 μL of L (10 μM) labelled with amino group were dropwise added into above mixed liquid for reacting at 4 °C overnight with stirring to obtain L/MBs by amide bond. After removing the excess L by magnetic separation, 20 μL of prepared S-DA solution and 10 μL of hairpin (H1, 10 μM) were immediately mixed into above solution. Then, the mixer was reacted at 95 °C for 5 min followed by slowly cooled to 37 °C in the procedure of 0.5 °C/min and then maintained at 37 °C for 2 h to form pinwheel-shaped DNA structure (H1/S/L/MBs). Finally, after magnetic separation, the prepared DNA structure was stored in TAE buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal amplification of the localized catalytic hairpin assembly (L-CHA)
ce:section.ce:para: The processes of signal amplification were as follows. Primarily, 10 μL of miRNA-222 (100 pM–100 aM) and10 μL of hairpin H2 (10 μM) were mixed into the prepared pinwheel-shaped DNA structure at 37 °C for 2 h to obtain the reaction solution, in which the hairpin H1 was opened by the miRNA-222 to show toehold for further hybridization. Subsequently, the hybridization of H1 and H2 by strand displacement reaction could release miRNA-222 to take part in the next cycle and output DNA to construct biosensor.

Firstly, the bare GCE was cleaned based on the method described as previously reported (Yang et al., 2023) Then, 10 μL of PCDs solution was dropped on the pretreated GCE to dry at 37 °C for 1 h, next 10 μL of Au NPs was added on the modified GCE to dry at 37 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of capture DNA (cp DNA, 1 μM) was reacted at 4 °C overnight linked by Au–S bond. After separating the unreacted cp DNA with deionized water, 1% BSA (10 μL) was incubated on the modified GCE for 30 min at 25 °C to block the non-specific sites. At last, above assembled GCE was rinsed to remove the unbonded material by deionized water and reserved at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology characterization of the prepared PCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the synthesized polymerized carbon dots (PCDs) was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image with the scale of 20 and 10 nm respectively. As described in Fig. 1 A–B, PCDs with particle size of 3.65 ± 0.5 nm was evenly dispersed. Furthermore, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images inserted in Fig. 1B indicated that PCDs showed well-resolved lattice planes of 0.20 nm which was consistent with the (100) facets of graphite confirming the formation of sp2 graphitic cores (Zhang et al., 2023b). Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S2A) showed two broad diffraction peaks at 28.8° and 41.5° corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of PCDs, which suggested a low level of disordered carbons with incomplete carbonization (Tang et al., 2023). Meanwhile, several different peaks were presented in the Raman spectrum in Fig. S2B, thus further proved that the PCDs contained several different carbon species (Ye et al., 2019). Furthermore, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image (Fig. 1C) implied the excellent dispersion of PCDs and the white line of the AFM (Fig. 1D) manifested that the high profile was 2.5 nm, suggesting the constitution of PCDs by multiple layers of graphene.

In addition, the surface chemical properties and element types of PCDs were further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). In Fig. 2 A, the XPS showed three significant values at 285.83 eV, 398.89 eV, 532.73 eV for the characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, respectively. Moreover, the high-resolution of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) displayed five fitted peaks at 284.93 eV (C–C/CC), 286.46 eV (C–O/C–N), 287.52 eV (CO), 292.96 eV and 295.77 eV (π-π). Fig. 2C depicted that the characteristic peaks of N 1s element at 398.43 eV (pyridinic-N) and 399.89 eV (graphitic N) were formed by N doping carbonization. Furthermore, two characteristic peaks of CO (531.95 eV) and C–O (533 eV) were observed in Fig. 2D, which was consistent with the corresponding outcomes of the C 1s spectrum. The above experimental results indicated that the synthesized PCDs had many functional groups (pyridinic-N, graphitic N, amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, aldehyde and CC), among which there not only had newly formed functional groups, but also retained part of the raw materials, so it is proved that PCDs produced additional surface states by N and O doping.

In order to further prove that N and O element was successfully doped at PCDs, the following experiments were conducted. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) and ultraviolet absorption spectrum (UV) were performed to analyze different CDs synthesized from different raw materials (See Supporting information for the synthesis steps of different CDs). As seen in Fig. 3 A, compared with CDs synthesized by 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydroxybenzene, PCDs showed several new unique characteristic peaks at 1095, 1518, 1587, 3301 cm−1, indicating the stretching vibrations of N functional groups (N–N, CN, C–N–C and N–H), respectively. More importantly, in fingerprint region, the specific peaks of C–C vibration of the polyaromatic structure showed more obvious peaks in the spectra of PCDs, which attributed to that the doped nitrogen atoms enhanced the dipole moment of the bonds to significantly increase the absorption (Chen et al., 2019). Furthermore, UV–Vis absorption spectrum of OP-CDs synthesized by OPDA, DA-CDs synthesized by DAH and PCDs was presented in Fig. 3B, the OP-CDs (curve a) exhibited three absorption peaks located at 266, 270 and 448 nm corresponding to π→π* transition absorption of the aromatic CC and n→π* transition of CO, CN, respectively. DA-CDs (curve b) appeared an absorption peak at 287 nm due to π→π* transition absorption of the aromatic CC. PCDs (curve c) featured π→π* transition absorption of the aromatic CC bond located at 266 and 270 nm, and broad peak ranging from 476 to 636 nm, ascribing to the large conjugated π-system. The n→π* transition of PCDs was redshifted and the intensity of absorption peak was obviously enhanced compared to those of OP-CDs and DA-CDs, because larger conjugated π-system was formed by the polymerization of raw materials and the N and O doping to introduce more functional groups in surface and plentiful n orbital energy levels between the π→π* transition to narrow the gap distance for photon transition (Zhang et al., 2023a). All the above results proved the successful doping of N and O in the PCDs.

In order to study the optical properties of PCDs, fluorescence (FL) and ECL spectra were measured. As seen in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence emission peak of PCDs was at 635 nm under the excitation wavelength of 560 nm and the ECL emission peak appeared at 675 nm. By observing the two groups of wavelengths, it was obvious that the ECL emission wavelength red shifted 45 nm, which may be attributed to the fact that ECL emission were determined by surface states. Further, ECL 3D color map of PCDs had been used in our work to study the evolution and devolution of ECL behavior. It can be seen from Fig. 3D that under the scanning voltage of 0 to −2 V, the maximum ECL emission wavelength was located at 675 nm with the strongest ECL intensity when the voltage value was closed to −2 V, which demonstrated that the ECL intensity of PCDs depended on the voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement mechanism of ECL binary (PCDs/S2O8 2−) system
ce:section.ce:para:
To find out the ECL enhancement mechanism of PCDs, the ECL and electrochemical test were carried out. As seen in Fig. 3E and F, the CV and ECL potential curves of the GCE electrode were recorded in different situation. Firstly, it can be seen from the curve a that the bare GCE had almost no ECL peak and reduction peak in 0.1 M PBS, while bare GCE in the 0.1 M PBS containing 10 mM S2O8 2− (curve b) showed a ECL signal at 4524 a.u. Appreciably, bare GCE presented a reduction peak at −0.8 V in S2O8 2− solution, indicating that S2O8 2− was reduced to SO4 •− (Equation (1)). Furthermore, PCDs modified GCE (curve c) showed a weak ECL peak and a reduction peak at −1.5 V in 0.1 M PBS, revealing that PCDs could be electrochemically reduced to PCDs•− (Equation (3)) by injecting electrons into the conduction band (N. Gao et al., 2022). Besides, ECL intensity of PCDs/GCE (curve d) remarkably increased to 15017 a.u. and reduction peak clearly moved to −1.1 V in 0.1 M PBS containing 10 mM S2O8 2−, which demonstrated that the generation of PCDs•+ (Equation (2)) by oxidizing PCDs with SO4 •− probably occurred ahead of the generation of PCDs•− by electrochemically reducing PCDs at the cathode. Therefore, the strong ECL emission of PCDs in the S2O8 2− system was might ascribed to the strong high-energy annihilation between PCDs•+ and PCDs•− (Equation (4)). The enhancement mechanism of ECL Binary (PCDs/S2O8 2−) system was inferred as follows:
(1) S2O8 2− + e− → SO4 2− + SO4 •−
(2) PCDs + SO4 •− → SO4 2− + PCDs•+
(3) PCDs + e− → PCDs•−
(4) PCDs•+ + PCDs•− → PCDs *
(5) PCDs * → PCDs + hv

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility analysis of L-CHA strategy
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of meliorative L-CHA strategy was verified by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE). According to Fig. 4 , miRNA-222 (T), S, H1, H2 and L appeared an obvious band in lanes 1, 2, 3, 4 and 10, respectively. Compared to migration rate of the lane 4, a slower migration rate in lane 5 confirmed the successful formation of L/H1/S composite structures. Then, a new bright band appeared in the lane 6, which confirmed the formation of L/H1/S/T. Furthermore, with the participation of target miRNA-222, a new bright band appeared in lane 7. However, lane 8 only existed the H1/S and H2 bands in the absence of target, which proved the successful realization of the CHA strategy. Interestingly, many continuous bright bands appeared at the top of lane 9, compared to that of lane 10, proving that L can combined with different number of H1. To sum up, the successful realization of L-CHA strategy can be verified by the PAGE experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the biosensor to detect miRNA-222
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions (details of condition optimization in Supporting Information Fig. S4), miRNA-222 with various concentration was quantitatively detected by the designed biosensor. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, the ECL intensity rose as the concentration of miRNA-222 decreased from 100 pM to 100 aM with the linear regression equation of ΔI = 12609.19 + 735.92 lg c (ΔI refers to the change value from blank to obtained, R = 0.998) and detection limit of 76 aM (the calculation process could be found in Supporting Information), suggesting the distinct sensitivity of the constructed biosensor. As shown in Table S3, the designed biosensor had a lower detection limit and a wider detection range than those of other biosensors in terms of miRNA detection.

Three other miRNAs (miRNA-21, miRNA-221, miRNA-141) were taken to replace miRNA-222 for evaluating the selectivity of the designed biosensor. Fig. 5C showed that the ECL intensity of the three interference samples (10 pM, miRNA-141, 10 pM, miRNA-221, 10 pM, miRNA-21, respectively) were only slightly different from those of the blank sample, and the ECL intensity of the mixed sample (10 pM, miRNA-141, 10 pM, miRNA-221, 10 pM, miRNA-21 and 1 pM, miRNA-222) was close to that of miRNA miRNA-222 (1 pM), indicating the outstanding selectivity of the biosensor. Besides, the RSD values (Fig. 5D) of the ECL response with three different concentrations by consecutive 10 cycles cyclic voltammetry scans were all less than 4%, which proved the excellent stability of the biosensor. The experimental results meant that the biosensor had good selectivity and stability, which suggested that the biosensor could be used for further detection in real sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of designed biosensors in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrated that the biosensor for miRNA-222 could be further applied in practical samples, the cell lysates of MHCC-97L and HeLa were used to demonstrate the expression level of miRNA-222 in cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 5E, the ECL intensity reduced dramatically when the cells number of MHCC-97L increased from 1 × 101 to 1 × 104, suggesting that miRNA-222 was high expressed in MHCC-97L. Nevertheless, the ECL intensity changed slightly with increasing the number of HeLa cell, suggesting the low expression of miRNA-222 in HeLa cells. This result fully complied with quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) (Fig. 5F) and previous literature (Wang et al., 2022) findings, implying that the biosensor had great potential to detection miRNA-222 in cancer cells.

To sum up, polymerized carbon dots (PCDs) as a novel ECL emitter was synthesized to exhibit many addtional surface functional groups and large conjugated π-system to improve electronic conductivity, reduce band gap and enhance radiation transition, which possess high ECL efficiency and long emission wavelength for further ECL biosensing. Prominently, an ultrasensitive biosensor based on PCDs as ECL emitter and the meliorative localized catalytic hairpin assembly (L-CHA) as amplifying apparatus was constructed for sensitive detection of microRNA 222. Meaningfully, the PCDs proposed in this work offers a innovative approach to find efficient ECL emitter for construction of biosensor in biomolecular detection, which is expected to be applied to clinical diagnosis.

Shuang Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yu-Ting Yang: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yi-Fei Chen: Supervision. Jia-Li Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Ya-Qin Chai: Writing – review & editing. Ruo Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the NNSF of China (22374122, 22174113, 22176153 and 22304141), Chongqing Postdoctoral Natural Science Foundation (CSTB2023NSCQ-BHX0009) and Chongqing Yousheng technology development Co., LTD.
